text: A thirty-eight years old female presented with symptoms of dysmenorrhea and infertility. On ultrasound examination, bilateral endometriomas were found and thereby the decision for laparoscopy was taken.
The laparoscopic view reveals a frozen pelvis with adhesions involving bowel loops, indicating extensive scar tissue formation and anatomical distortion.
The laparoscopic view reveals that the uterus, bilateral fallopian tubes, and ovaries are not visible due to the presence of adhesions.
The surgeon initiates adhesiolysis, beginning the process of separating adhesions within the pelvic region.
The surgeon performs adhesiolysis using traction and counter traction techniques, manipulating the tissue to separate adhesions within the pelvic cavity.
A neutralization should always be started from the normal area proceeding towards the abnormal to helping clear identification of the planes.
The surgeon applies traction, revealing a white line between two structures that indicates a clear plane of dissection in the frozen pelvis.
During adhesiolysis for endometriosis, the surgeon carefully spares the peritoneum while separating adhesions.
The surgeon uses cold scissors to perform adhesiolysis, minimizing lateral thermal damage to surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses cold scissors to dissect in the correct planes, carefully avoiding adhesions and preventing blood loss while ensuring no injury to adjacent structures.

The surgeon visualizes the uterus and injects vasopressin into the myelodendron, using a dilution of twenty units in one hundred ml or forty units in two hundred ml, to aid in adhesiolysis and reduce blood loss.
